subject: Physics
q: A point $\mathrm{P}$ moves in counter-clockwise direction on a circular path as shown in the figure. The movement of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' is such that it sweeps out a length $s=t^3+5$, where $s$ is in metres and $t$ is in seconds. The radius of the path is $20 \mathrm{~m}$. The acceleration of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' when $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is nearly
question: A point $\mathrm{P}$ moves in counter-clockwise direction on a circular path as shown in the figure. The movement of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' is such that it sweeps out a length $s=t^3+5$, where $s$ is in metres and $t$ is in seconds. The radius of the path is $20 \mathrm{~m}$. The acceleration of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' when $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is nearly
solution:
$$
S=t^3+5
$$
$\therefore \quad$ speed, $\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{ds}}{\mathrm{dt}}=3 \mathrm{t}^2$
and rate of change of speed $=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=6 \mathrm{t}$
$\therefore \quad$ tangential acceleration at $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}=6 \times 2=12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
at $t=2 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{v}=3(2)^2=12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore \quad$ centripetal acceleration, $\quad a_c=\frac{v^2}{R}=\frac{144}{20} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
$\therefore \quad$ net acceleration $=\sqrt{\mathrm{a}_t^2+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}}^2}$ $\approx 14 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

question: In the given figure, two wheels P and Q are connected by a belt B. The radius of P is three times that of Q. In the case of the same rotational kinetic energy, the ratio of rotational inertias I1I2 will be x: 1 . The value of x will be ______.
solution:
12I1ω12=12I2ω22

I1v3R2=I2vR2

I1I2=91

subject: Physics
q: a string is wound around a hollow cylinder of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ and radius $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$. If the string is now pulled with a horizontal force of $40 \mathrm{~N}$, and the cylinder is rolling without slipping on a horizontal surface (see figure), then the angular acceleration of the cylinder will be (Neglect the mass and thickness of the string)
question: a string is wound around a hollow cylinder of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ and radius $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$. If the string is now pulled with a horizontal force of $40 \mathrm{~N}$, and the cylinder is rolling without slipping on a horizontal surface (see figure), then the angular acceleration of the cylinder will be (Neglect the mass and thickness of the string)
solution: From newton's second law $40+\mathrm{f}=\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{R} \alpha)$ ...(i) Taking torque about 0 we get $40 \times \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{f} \times \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{I} \alpha$ $40 \times \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{f} \times \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{mR}^{2} \alpha$ $40-\mathrm{f}=\mathrm{mR} \alpha$ ...(ii) Solving equation (i) and (ii) $\alpha=\frac{40}{\mathrm{mR}}=16 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$

subject: Physics
q: The following bodies are made to roll up (without slipping) the same inclined plane from a horizontal plane: (i) a ring of radius <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> (ii) a solid cylinder of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and (iii) a solid sphere of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> If, in each case, the speed of the center of mass at the bottom of the incline is same, the ratio of the maximum heights they climb is:
question: The following bodies are made to roll up (without slipping) the same inclined plane from a horizontal plane: (i) a ring of radius <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> (ii) a solid cylinder of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> and (iii) a solid sphere of radius <math><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> If, in each case, the speed of the center of mass at the bottom of the incline is same, the ratio of the maximum heights they climb is:
solution:
From conservation of mechanical energy.
12mRv2+12IRωr2=mRghR⇒V22+V22=ghR
12mdv2+12Idωd2=mdghd⇒V22+V24=ghd
12msv2+12Isωs2=msghs⇒V22+V25=ghs
∴  hR:hd:hs=1:34:710=20:15:14

The moment of Inertia of the circular plate about an axis perpendicular to the plate and passing through its edge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>M</mi><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The value of the coefficient <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is:
question: A thin circular plate of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> has its density varying as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext mathvariant="italic">ρ</mtext><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><msub><mtext>=ρ</mtext><mtext>0</mtext></msub><mtext mathvariant="italic">r</mtext></math> with <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> as constant and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is the distance from its centre. The moment of Inertia of the circular plate about an axis perpendicular to the plate and passing through its edge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>M</mi><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The value of the coefficient <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is:
solution:
dm=2πrdr×ρ=2πρ0r2dr
M=2πρ0∫0Rr2dr=23πρ0R3
ICM=ρdmr2=2πρ0∫r4dr=25πρ0R5
Icm=35MR2
I=Icm+MR2=85MR2

subject: Physics
q:
A wheel of radius 0.2 m rotates freely about its center when a string that is wrapped over its rim is pulled by force of 10 N as shown in figure. The established torque produces an angular acceleration of $2 \mathrm{~rad} / \mathrm{s}^2$. Moment of inertia of the wheel is _____ $\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^2$.
(Acceleration due to gravity $=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
question:
A wheel of radius 0.2 m rotates freely about its center when a string that is wrapped over its rim is pulled by force of 10 N as shown in figure. The established torque produces an angular acceleration of $2 \mathrm{~rad} / \mathrm{s}^2$. Moment of inertia of the wheel is _____ $\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^2$.
(Acceleration due to gravity $=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{FR}=\mathrm{I} \alpha \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{FR}}{\alpha}=\frac{10 \times 0.2}{2}=1 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^2\end{aligned}$

question: Consider a situation in which a ring, a solid cylinder and a solid sphere roll down on the same inclined plane without slipping. Assume that they start rolling from rest and having identical diameter. The correct statement for this situation is

The ring has the greatest and the cylinder has the least velocity of the centre of mass at the bottom of the inclined plane.
All of them will have same velocity.
The cylinder has the greatest and the sphere has the least velocity of the centre of mass at the bottom of the inclined plane.
solution:

⇒aring <asolidcylinder <asolidsphere 
⇒vring <vsolidcylinder <vsolidsphere

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a uniform speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Y</mi></math> plane along the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. The angular momentum of the particle about the origin will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a uniform speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Y</mi></math> plane along the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. The angular momentum of the particle about the origin will be _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Equation of line given: y-x-4=0.

Let d1 be the perpendicular distance of given line from origin.

d1=-412+12=22

So, |L→|=mvd1=5×32×22 kg m2 s-1

=60 kg m2 s-1

subject: Physics
q: Two discs have moments of intertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> about their respective axes perpendicular to the plane and passing through the centre. They are rotating with angular speeds, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively and are brought into contact face to face with their axes of rotation coaxial. The loss in kinetic energy of the system in the process is given by:
question: Two discs have moments of intertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> about their respective axes perpendicular to the plane and passing through the centre. They are rotating with angular speeds, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively and are brought into contact face to face with their axes of rotation coaxial. The loss in kinetic energy of the system in the process is given by:
solution:
Angular momentum conservation

I1ω1+I2ω2=I1+I2ω

ω=I1ω1+I2ω2I1+I2

Loss =12I1ω12+12I2ω22-12I1+I2ω2

=12I1ω12+12I2ω22-12I1+I2I1ω1+I2ω2I1+I22

=12I1I2I1+I2ω1-ω22

Ei-Ef=I1I2ω1-ω222I1+I2

The angular speed of the ring will become:
question: A thin circular ring of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is rotating about its axis with an angular speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Two particles having mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> each are now attached at diametrically opposite points. The angular speed of the ring will become:
solution:
Using conservation of angular momentum
⇒Mr2ω=Mr2+2mr2ω'

⇒ω'=MωM+2 m.

The moment of inertia and angular velocity of the first disc are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mtext> rad </mtext><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively while those for the second one are  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mtext> </mtext></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. At some instant they get stuck together and start rotating as a single system about their common axis with some angular speed. The kinetic energy of the combined system is :
question: Two uniform circular discs are rotating independently in the same direction around their common axis passing through their centres. The moment of inertia and angular velocity of the first disc are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mtext> rad </mtext><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively while those for the second one are  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mtext> </mtext></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. At some instant they get stuck together and start rotating as a single system about their common axis with some angular speed. The kinetic energy of the combined system is :
solution:
Common angular velocity from angular momentum conversation

ω=l1ω1+I2ω2I1+I2=0.1×10+0.2×50.1+0.2=203
Final K.E.
Kf=12I1ω2+12I2ω2
=120.1+0.2×2032⇒kf=203

question: A cylindrical vessel containing a liquid is rotated about its axis so that the liquid rises at its sides as shown in the figure. The radius of vessel is 5 cm and the angular speed of rotation is ω rad s-1. The difference in the height, h in cm of liquid at the Centre of vessel and at the sides of the vessel will be :
solution:
ρdrω2r=ρgdh

ω2∫0Rr dr=g∫0hdh

ω2R22=gh

 h=ω2R22g=25ω22g cm.

question: A square Lamina OABC of length 10 cm is pivoted at 'O'. Forces act at Lamina as shown in figure. If Lamina remains stationary, then the magnitude of F is :
solution:
Since the lamina is equilibrium.
$\therefore \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{net}}=0 \& \tau_{\mathrm{net}}=0$

$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{o}}=10 \ell-\mathrm{F} \ell \Rightarrow \mathrm{F}=10 \mathrm{~N}$

q: The moment of inertial of a solid sphere, about an axis parallel to its diameter and at a distance of <math><mi>x</mi></math> from it, is <math><mi>′</mi><mi>I</mi><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mi>′</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Which one of the graphs represents the variation of <math><mi>I</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> with <math><mi>x</mi></math> correctly?
question: The moment of inertial of a solid sphere, about an axis parallel to its diameter and at a distance of <math><mi>x</mi></math> from it, is <math><mi>′</mi><mi>I</mi><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mi>′</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Which one of the graphs represents the variation of <math><mi>I</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> with <math><mi>x</mi></math> correctly?
solution:
we know that by se the parallel axis theorem :-

Moment of inertia about a point which is parallel of the axis  and at a distance of  X given as 

Ix=Icm+mx2

Ix=25mR2+mx2  ⇒  Parabola opening upward

subject: Physics
q: Consider a disc of mass 5 kg, radius 2 m, rotating with angular velocity of 10 rad s-1 about an axis perpendicular to the plane of rotation. An identical disc is kept gently over the rotating disc along the same axis. The energy dissipated so that both the discs continue to rotate together without slipping is _________J.
question: Consider a disc of mass 5 kg, radius 2 m, rotating with angular velocity of 10 rad s-1 about an axis perpendicular to the plane of rotation. An identical disc is kept gently over the rotating disc along the same axis. The energy dissipated so that both the discs continue to rotate together without slipping is _________J.
solution:
Initial angular momentum, Li=Iωi=MR22·ω=100 kg m2 s-1

Initial kinetic energy, Ei=12·MR22·ω2=500 J

From conservation of angular momentum, we can write

L→i=L→f⇒100=2Iωf

⇒ωf=5 rad s-1

Ef=2×12·5(2)22·(5)2=250 J

Therefore, required energy loss ΔE=250 J

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass m=1 kg slides with velocity v=6 m s-1 on a frictionless horizontal surface and collides with a uniform vertical rod and sticks to it as shown. The rod is pivoted about O and swings as a result of the collision making angle θ before momentarily coming to rest. if the rod has mass M=2 kg, and length ℓ=1 m, the value of θ is approximately(take g=10 m s-2
question: A block of mass m=1 kg slides with velocity v=6 m s-1 on a frictionless horizontal surface and collides with a uniform vertical rod and sticks to it as shown. The rod is pivoted about O and swings as a result of the collision making angle θ before momentarily coming to rest. if the rod has mass M=2 kg, and length ℓ=1 m, the value of θ is approximately(take g=10 m s-2
solution:
Angular momentum

mvl=ml2+2ml23ω

mvl=53ml2ω

ω=3v5l

12Iω2=2mgl21-cos θ+mgl1-cos θ

1253ml29v225l2=2mgl 1-cos θ

35×2mv2=2mgl1-cosθ

310×362×10=1-cos θ

1-2750=cos θ

cos θ=2350=0.46

θ=cos-10.46=63°

subject: Physics
q: A smooth wire of length 2πr is bent into a circle and kept in a vertical plane. A bead can slide smoothly on the wire. When the circle is rotating with angular speed ω about the vertical diameter AB, as shown in figure, the bead is at rest with respect to the circular ring at position P as shown. Then the value of ω2 is equal to:
question: A smooth wire of length 2πr is bent into a circle and kept in a vertical plane. A bead can slide smoothly on the wire. When the circle is rotating with angular speed ω about the vertical diameter AB, as shown in figure, the bead is at rest with respect to the circular ring at position P as shown. Then the value of ω2 is equal to:
solution:
From free body diagram of bead,
Ncos30°=mg .... (1)
Nsin30°=mr2ω2 ..... (2)
By dividing equation (2) by equation (1)
tan30° = rω22g
ω2 = 2gr3

The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A metre scale is balanced on a knife edge at its centre. When two coins, each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> are put one on the top of the other at the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> mark the scale is found to be balanced at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> mark. The mass of the metre scale is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Let the mass of the scale be m.

The centre of mass of the scale will be at 50 cm.

Applying torque balance, 

0=mg×50-40-2×10g×40-10⇒m=60 g=6×10-2 kg

subject: Physics
q: A solid spherical ball is rolling on a frictionless horizontal plane surface about its axis of symmetry. The ratio of rotational kinetic energy of the ball to its total kinetic energy is
question: A solid spherical ball is rolling on a frictionless horizontal plane surface about its axis of symmetry. The ratio of rotational kinetic energy of the ball to its total kinetic energy is
solution:
Under pure rolling condition: v=ωR

Translational K.E=12mv2

Rotational K.E=12×25mR2vR2=15mv2

Total K.E=12mv2+15mv2=710mv2

So, Rotational K·ETotal K·E=15mv2710mv2=27

subject: Physics
q: The pulley shown in figure is made using a thin rim and two rods of length equal to diameter of the rim. The rim and each rod have a mass of $M$. Two blocks of mass of $M$ and $m$ are attached to two ends of a light string passing over the pulley, which is hinged to rotate freely in vertical plane about its center. The magnitudes of the acceleration experienced by the blocks is $\_\_\_\_$ (assume no slipping of string on pulley).
question: The pulley shown in figure is made using a thin rim and two rods of length equal to diameter of the rim. The rim and each rod have a mass of $M$. Two blocks of mass of $M$ and $m$ are attached to two ends of a light string passing over the pulley, which is hinged to rotate freely in vertical plane about its center. The magnitudes of the acceleration experienced by the blocks is $\_\_\_\_$ (assume no slipping of string on pulley).
solution:
The pulley consists of a rim (mass $M$) and two rods (each mass $M$) forming a diameter structure.
The moment of inertia is: $I = I_{rim} + I_{rods} = MR^2 + 2 \times \frac{MR^2}{3} = \frac{5MR^2}{3}$.
For the Atwood pulley system, applying Newton's second law:
Mass $m$ (upward): $T_1 = m(g-a)$
Mass $M$ (downward): $T_2 = M(g+a)$
Pulley rotation: $T_2 R - T_1 R = I\alpha = \frac{5MR^2}{3} \cdot \frac{a}{R}$
Thus: $T_2 - T_1 = \frac{5Ma}{3}$
Substituting: $M(g+a) - m(g-a) = \frac{5Ma}{3}$
$(M-m)g + (M+m)a = \frac{5Ma}{3}$
$(M-m)g = a\left(\frac{5M}{3} - M - m\right) = a\left(\frac{2M}{3} - m\right)$
$a = \frac{(M-m)g}{\frac{2M-3m}{3}} = \frac{3(M-m)g}{2M+3m}$, which is option (2).

question: Two identical spherical balls of mass M and radius R each are stuck on two ends of a rod of length 2R and mass M(see figure). The moment of inertia of the system about the axis passing perpendicularly through the centre of the rod is
solution:
Applying parallel axis theorem for the balls,
Ib=25MR2+M2R2


Itotal=2Ib+IR
Itotal=25MR2+M2R2×2+M122R2

Itotal=13715MR2

subject: Physics
q: A solid cylinder having radius $R$ and length $L$ is slipping on a rough horizontal plane. At time $t=0$ the cylinder has a translational velocity $v_0=49$ m/s, perpendicular to its axis and a rotational velocity $v_0/4R$ about the centre. The time taken by the cylinder to start rolling is ________ seconds. (coefficient of kinetic friction $\mu_K=0.25$ and $g=9.8$ m/s$^2$)
question: A solid cylinder having radius $R$ and length $L$ is slipping on a rough horizontal plane. At time $t=0$ the cylinder has a translational velocity $v_0=49$ m/s, perpendicular to its axis and a rotational velocity $v_0/4R$ about the centre. The time taken by the cylinder to start rolling is ________ seconds. (coefficient of kinetic friction $\mu_K=0.25$ and $g=9.8$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Initial translational velocity, $v_0 = 49$ m/s.

Initial angular velocity, $\omega_0 = \dfrac{v_0}{4R}$.

Velocity of the lowest point of the cylinder is $v_{bottom} = v_0 - \omega_0 R = v_0 - \dfrac{v_0}{4} = \dfrac{3v_0}{4} > 0$.

Since the lowest point is slipping forward, kinetic friction $f_k = \mu_k m g$ acts in the backward direction.

Translational acceleration, $a = -\dfrac{f_k}{m} = -\mu_k g$.

Angular acceleration, $\alpha = \dfrac{f_k R}{I} = \dfrac{\mu_k m g R}{\dfrac{1}{2} m R^2} = \dfrac{2\mu_k g}{R}$.

At time $t$, the translational velocity is $v = v_0 - \mu_k g t$.

The angular velocity is $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t = \dfrac{v_0}{4R} + \dfrac{2\mu_k g}{R} t$.

For pure rolling to start, $v = \omega R$.

$v_0 - \mu_k g t = \left( \dfrac{v_0}{4R} + \dfrac{2\mu_k g}{R} t \right) R$

$v_0 - \mu_k g t = \dfrac{v_0}{4} + 2\mu_k g t$

$3\mu_k g t = \dfrac{3v_0}{4}$

$t = \dfrac{v_0}{4\mu_k g}$

Substituting the given values $v_0 = 49$ m/s, $\mu_k = 0.25$, and $g = 9.8$ m/s$^2$:

$t = \dfrac{49}{4 \times 0.25 \times 9.8} = \dfrac{49}{9.8} = 5$ s.

Answer: $5$

subject: Physics
q: A fly wheel having mass 3 kg and radius 5 m is free to rotate about a horizontal axis. A string having negligible mass is wound around the wheel and the loose end of the string is connected to 3 kg mass. The mass is kept at rest initially and released. Kinetic energy of the wheel when the mass descends by 3 m is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{J}.\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
question: A fly wheel having mass 3 kg and radius 5 m is free to rotate about a horizontal axis. A string having negligible mass is wound around the wheel and the loose end of the string is connected to 3 kg mass. The mass is kept at rest initially and released. Kinetic energy of the wheel when the mass descends by 3 m is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{J}.\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
solution:
Let the mass of the flywheel be $M = 3$ kg and its radius be $R = 5$ m.
Let the hanging mass be $m = 3$ kg and the distance descended be $h = 3$ m.
The flywheel is a disk, so its moment of inertia is $I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$.
By conservation of energy, the loss in potential energy of the hanging mass equals the gain in kinetic energy of the system.
$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$
Since the string does not slip, $v = R\omega$, so $\omega = \frac{v}{R}$.
$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}(\frac{1}{2}MR^2)(\frac{v}{R})^2$
$mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 + \frac{1}{4}Mv^2$
Substituting the values: $3 \times 10 \times 3 = \frac{1}{2}(3)v^2 + \frac{1}{4}(3)v^2$
$90 = \frac{3}{2}v^2 + \frac{3}{4}v^2 = \frac{9}{4}v^2$
$v^2 = \frac{90 \times 4}{9} = 40$ m$^2$/s$^2$.
The kinetic energy of the wheel is $K_{wheel} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \frac{1}{4}Mv^2$.
$K_{wheel} = \frac{1}{4} \times 3 \times 40 = 30$ J.

frR=Iα=mR22α …(ii)

From i, (ii) and (iii)

F-mR α2=mαR

F=32mR  α

α=2F3mR

q: A thin smooth rod of length <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> and mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is rotating freely with angular speed <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> about an axis perpendicular to the rod and passing through center. Two beads of mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and negligible size are at the center of the rod initially. The beads of mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and negligible size are at the center of the rod initially. The beads are free to slide along the rod. The angular speed of the system, when the beads reach the opposite ends of the rod, will be:
question: A thin smooth rod of length <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> and mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is rotating freely with angular speed <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> about an axis perpendicular to the rod and passing through center. Two beads of mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and negligible size are at the center of the rod initially. The beads of mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and negligible size are at the center of the rod initially. The beads are free to slide along the rod. The angular speed of the system, when the beads reach the opposite ends of the rod, will be:
solution:
From conservation of angular momentum.
ML212ω0=ML212+mL22+mL22ω'
ML212ω0=ML212+mL22ω'
ω'=Mω0M+6m

subject: Physics
q: A solid cylinder is released from rest from the top of an inclined plane of inclination 30° and length 60 cm. If the cylinder rolls without slipping, its speed upon reaching the bottom of the inclined plane is ______ m s-1. (Given g=10 m s-2)
question: A solid cylinder is released from rest from the top of an inclined plane of inclination 30° and length 60 cm. If the cylinder rolls without slipping, its speed upon reaching the bottom of the inclined plane is ______ m s-1. (Given g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Let speed of cylinder upon reaching the bottom of the inclined plane be v.

Here, gain in kinetic energy=loss in potential energy

So, 12Iω2+12mv2=mglsinθ

⇒12mr22ω2+12mv2=mglsin30°

For pure rolling, v=rω

⇒12mr22vr2+12mv2=mglsin30°

⇒14mv2+12mv2=mglsin30°⇒34mv2=mgl2⇒v=4gl6

Putting the values, we have

⇒v=4×10×0.66=2 m s-1

q: M and R be the mass and radius of a disc. A small disc of radius $R / 3$ is removed from the bigger disc as shown in figure. The moment of inertia of remaining part of bigger disc about an axis AB passing through the centre O and perpendicular to the plane of disc is $\frac{4}{x} M R^2$. The value of $x$ is ________.
question: M and R be the mass and radius of a disc. A small disc of radius $R / 3$ is removed from the bigger disc as shown in figure. The moment of inertia of remaining part of bigger disc about an axis AB passing through the centre O and perpendicular to the plane of disc is $\frac{4}{x} M R^2$. The value of $x$ is ________.
solution:
Without cavity $\mathrm{I}_1=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{2}$
$\begin{aligned}
\text { Mass of removed disc }= & \frac{\mathrm{M}}{\pi \mathrm{R}^2} \times\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{3}\right)^2 \pi \\ & =\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{9}\right)
\end{aligned}$
M.I. of removed disc $I_2=\frac{\frac{M}{9}\left(\frac{R}{3}\right)^2}{2}+\frac{M}{9} \times\left(\frac{2 R}{3}\right)^2$
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{18} \\ & \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_1-\mathrm{I}_2=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{18}=\frac{4 \mathrm{MR}^2}{9} \\ & (\mathrm{n}=9)
\end{aligned}$
